mappings.rationaleAI: Endocrine system physiology covering insulin, growth hormone, and pituitary cascades directly addresses hormonal coordination (C), vertebrate endocrine structure and function (C.2.b), relationships between endocrine and neural regulation (C.1), evolutionary aspects (C.4), and circadian hormone adjustment relates to biological clock periodicity (E).

Neuroanatomy of cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, and spinal cord addresses the vertebrate nervous system (D.4.a), ion channels and sensory processing relate to nerve impulse transmission (D.2.c.ii), sensory information processing (B.2, B.3), and brain biodynamics (D.4.e). Rhythmic brain activity relates to biological clock rhythms without external correlates (E.1).

Retinal photoreceptors, lens, and optic nerve connections constitute a photoreceptor sensory system (B.1.b), with evolution of color vision across species illustrating evolution of sensory systems (B.2).

Genetically programmed molecular clocks governing near-24-hour cycles directly address the biological clock (E), rhythms correlated with geophysical cycles including solar-day rhythms (E.2), molecular mechanisms of biological oscillators (E.3), and factors including light entrainment affecting periodicities (E.4).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationaleAI: Sleep mechanisms including REM/NREM stages relate to dormancy and reduced metabolic states (A.6), circadian regulation through melatonin addresses the biological clock (E), and sleep-wake cycles correlate with solar-day rhythms (E.2).

Homeostasis and internal parameter regulation directly address maintenance of steady states (A.1, A.2), sensory perception relates to information reception (B), and the human nervous system (D.5) and endocrine system (C.5) are covered as parts of physiological regulation.

Neural basis of perception, attention, and memory processing addresses sensory information processing (B.2, B.3), and brain imaging of cognitive components relates to vertebrate central nervous system structure (D.4.a).

How sensory information guides behaviour covers classification of sensory systems (B.1), evolution of perceptual systems across species (B.2), and interactions between sense cells and sensory neurons (B.3).

Olfactory neural processing across species constitutes a chemoreceptor sensory system (B.1.b), comparative olfactory systems illustrate evolution of sensory systems (B.2), and genetic underpinnings of smell perception address sensory information interactions (B.3).

Nervous system features across phyla including Cnidaria and Annelida address invertebrate and vertebrate nervous system diversity (D.3), and evolutionary relationships relate to evolution of the vertebrate nervous system (D.4.d).
Insect endocrine systems governing development relate to invertebrate endocrine systems (C.3), and insect nervous system diversity addresses invertebrate nervous systems (D.3).

Action potential mechanisms and sympathetic nervous system responses relate to nerve impulse transmission (D.2.c.ii), and spinal cord function addresses the vertebrate peripheral nervous system (D.4.b).
Neural correlates of consciousness and mechanisms of attention address sensory information processing (B) and biodynamics of the vertebrate nervous system (D.4.e).
Neuroscience and physiology of pain perception addresses mechanoreceptor and nociceptor sensory systems (B.1.b) and neural processing of sensory pain information (B.3).
